The probability that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A is found by analyzing the difference between two normal distributions where the variance of the difference equals the sum of the individual variances. The mean of the difference is 5 and the standard deviation of the difference is approximately 15.62. This calculation yields a probability of roughly 0.63.

Regarding the performance improvement the exponential growth function for Group A and the logistic growth function for Group B intersect at a specific time point during the semester. Solving the equation where both functions equal each other requires numerical methods because an algebraic solution is not straightforward. The process of finding this intersection involves iterating through time values until the outputs match within a small tolerance. Nothing prevents us from stating that the explanation for this intersection relies on the different growth rates and saturation points of the two models. The force of the logistic model eventually slows its growth while the exponential model continues to grow without bound in this simplified context. The time t where both groups share the same performance level is approximately 18.5 units of time based on the provided parameters.
